paper_title: The Hidden Power of Scaling Factor in LoRA Optimization

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: In Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), the scaling factor $α$ is often treated as a mere complement to the learning rate, yet its role in optimization remains poorly understood. In this paper, we reveal that the scaling factor $α$ and the learning rate function differently, with $α$ emerging as the dominant driver of effective optimization, delivering gains that cannot be replicated by learning rate scaling alone. Through the synergy of extensive empirical analysis and a theoretical Signal-Drift framework, we uncover three findings into LoRA's scaling mechanism: First, LoRA's spectral suppression smooths the optimization landscape, rendering standard hyperparameters overly conservative and creating an optimization gap. Second, when leveraging this smoothness to accelerate convergence, $α$ outperforms the learning rate by amplifying the task signal without increasing the drift ratio. Third, the optimal scaling factor follows a sublinear relationship with the rank, well characterized by a square-root law with an unexpectedly large coefficient, revealing the insufficient scaling of existing rank-tied heuristics. Based on these insights, we propose LoRA-$α$, a minimalist framework that restores $α$ to its principled regime, making LoRA compatible with standard small learning rates. Extensive evaluations across diverse tasks demonstrate that LoRA-$α$ consistently improves performance while streamlining hyperparameter search, unleashing the learning potential of LoRA.

sections.content: In Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), the scaling factor α is often treated as mere complement to the learning rate, yet its role in optimization remains poorly understood. In this paper, we reveal that the scaling factor α and the learning rate function differently, with α emerging as the dominant driver of effective optimization, delivering gains that cannot be replicated by learning rate scaling alone. Through the synergy of extensive empirical analysis and theoretical Signal-Drift framework, we uncover three findings into LoRAs scaling mechanism: First, LoRAs spectral suppression smooths the optimization landscape, rendering standard hyperparameters overly conservative and creating an optimization gap. Second, when leveraging this smoothness to accelerate convergence, α outperforms the learning rate by amplifying the task signal without increasing the drift ratio. Third, the optimal scaling factor follows sublinear relationship with the rank, well characterized by square-root law with an unexpectedly large coefficient, revealing the insufficient scaling of existing rank-tied heuristics. Based on these insights, we propose LoRA-α, minimalist framework that restores α to its principled regime, making LoRA compatible with standard small learning rates. Extensive evaluations across diverse tasks demonstrate that LoRA-α consistently improves performance while streamlining hyperparameter search, unleashing the learning potential of LoRA.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: The rapid growth of large pretrained models [OpenAI Team, 2020, 2023; Meta Team, 2023; Qwen Team, 2023; DeepSeek Team, 2025] has made efficient adaptation central challenge, driving the development of Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods [Mangrulkar et al., 2023; Lester et al., 2021; He et al., 2022; Edalati et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2025a]. Among these, Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) [Hu et al., 2022] has emerged as dominant approach due to its efficiency and stability. LoRA parameterizes weight updates as = α BA, where low-rank factors and approximate the update with rank and scaling factor α. This simple formulation reinforced by framework support [Mangrulkar et al., 2023], has enabled widespread adoption from Natural Language Processing (NLP) [Liu et al., 2022; Ding et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2024] to multimodal generation [Guo et al., 2024; Blattmann et al., 2023; Ruiz et al., 2023]. Despite its conceptual simplicity, the optimization behavior of LoRA remains poorly understood due to its inherent bilinear architecture and the complex interplay of hyperparameters. The scaling factor α, originating from feature learning principles [Yang & Hu, 2021], aims to decouple the optimal hyperparameters from the rank selection, thereby streamlining hyperparameter search. While research has explored initialization [Wang et al., 2024b; Meng et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2025b] and Correspondence: zhangzicheng6@jd.com Preprint. learning rates [Biderman et al., 2024; Schulman & Lab, 2025; Chen et al., 2026], the scaling factor α remains systematically underexplored, typically tethered to simplistic rank-based heuristics, such as α = [Hu et al., 2022] or 2r [Biderman et al., 2024]. Consequently, α is often regarded as secondary alternative to the learning rate for scaling updates, obscuring its pivotal role in reducing hyperparameter search and, more fundamentally, in shaping the underlying optimization regime. In this work, we analyze the scaling mechanism of LoRA through joint empirical and theoretical study. Across extensive hyperparameter sweeps, we consistently observe that effective optimization hinges more on sufficiently large scaling factor α than on elevating the learning rate η. To understand this phenomenon, we develop Signal-Drift framework that delineates LoRAs optimization characteristics. In this view, the task-relevant signal and the bilinear-induced drift respond differently to α and η, providing principled explanation for our empirical observations. Together, these empirical and theoretical evidence lead to the following three key findings: Spectral Suppression Causes Scaling Misalignment. We find that LoRAs low-rank parameterization induces spectral suppression of the task Hessian, effectively smoothing the optimization landscape. While this improves stability, it also renders standard hyperparameters overly conservative, leading to significant optimization gap and motivating the aggressive scaling practices observed in prior work [Hayou et al., 2024b; Schulman & Lab, 2025; Zhang et al., 2025b]. α and η Play Fundamentally Different Roles. Empirically, we observe that increasing α consistently yields better convergence than increasing η. Our framework explains this by showing that α amplifies the task-aligned signal, whereas η amplifies both the signal and bilinear drift. As result, α acts as purity-preserving accelerator, enabling faster and more stable optimization. Optimal Scaling Follows Sublinear Law. We identify sublinear relationship between the optimal α and rank r, concisely characterized by square-root law with large scaling coefficient. This reveals that commonly used rank-tied heuristics [Hu et al., 2022; Biderman et al., 2024] operate in severely under-scaled regime. With proper scaling, LoRA can directly adopt the standard small learning rates used in Full Fine-Tuning (FFT), while achieving superior performance. Based on both empirical and theoretical findings, we identify key limitation in prevailing LoRA practices: commonly used heuristics restrict α to insufficient magnitudes, limiting LoRAs optimization capacity. To address this, we propose LoRA-α, which introduces large base value with square-root scaling law to restore α to principled regime. This formulation enables practitioners to bypass costly hyperparameter tuning and directly adopt standard FFT learning rates. Extensive experiments across model scales (184M12B), task domains (natural language, reasoning, multimodal), and training paradigms (supervised, contrastive, and reinforcement learning) show that larger α with standard small η consistently yields superior performance. In summary, our contributions include: Empirically, we establish three key findings regarding LoRAs optimization through extensive hyperparameter sweeps. We uncover that performance hinges critically on large scaling factor, exposing pitfall in current rank-tied heuristics that leave LoRAs fitting potential underutilized. Theoretically, we develop Signal-Drift framework to provide principled explanation of these findings. By characterizing Hessian spectral suppression, we identify α as better accelerator and derive square-root law that aligns LoRAs optimization regime with that of FFT. We propose LoRA-α, minimalist framework that elevates the scaling factor to its principled regime while adopting standard FFT learning rates. Across diverse models, tasks, and training paradigms, it significantly improves over LoRA, often reaching performance comparable to FFT.

sections.title: 2 An Empirical Study of LoRA Scaling
sections.content: To study the scaling behavior of LoRA, we perform systematic hyperparameter sweeps to characterize the interplay between the scaling factor α, learning rate η, and rank r. We focus on fitting behavior, isolating optimization dynamics from generalization effects such as implicit low-rank regularization [Jang et al., 2024] and task diversity. This enables clean analysis of the optimization landscape, while generalization is evaluated separately in Section 5. Optimization Setup and Metrics. Following Schulman & Lab [2025], we conduct Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on two model scales (Llama 3-1B and 8B [Meta Team, 2024]) and datasets (Tulu 3 [Lambert et al., 2025] and OpenThoughts [Guha et al., 2026]), training each configuration for 2 Figure 1: Hyperparameter analysis of Llama 3-1B on the Tulu 3 dataset. Increasing α lowers the optimal loss and shifts η downward. (b) Scaling paths defined in Eq. (2) relative to the baseline (ηFFT = 2 105, α0 = 16), where ηFFT denotes the optimal FFT learning rate. Varying α reaches lower-loss regimes that are inaccessible via η-tuning alone. (c) Optimal scaling factor α as function of rank r, evaluated at ηFFT. The observed sublinear trend and large magnitude of α challenge conventional scaling heuristics. one epoch with batch size of 32 using AdamW. For clarity, we report results on Llama 3-1B with Tulu 3, deferring the rest to Appendix D. To manage the large search space over α, η, and r, we use 100k-example subset of Tulu 3 with maximum token length = 1024, and reserve 10k examples as proxy evaluation set Dprox. Let θ(r, η, α) denote the trained parameters under given configuration. According to optimization theory [Nesterov, 2013], this reduced maximum eigenvalue lowers the gradient Lipschitz constant, thereby expanding the stable learning rate bound that η 2/λmax[HSignal]. To explain why α-scaling achieves superior fitting capacity compared to η-scaling, our decomposition reveals fundamental asymmetry between the two hyperparameters. Proposition 4 (Asymmetric Scaling). This derivation proves that conventional linear scaling is mis-specified. Instead, α should scale sublinearly with rank and grow substantially with model width under standard initialization.

Based on this insight, we propose LoRA-α to redefine LoRA as: = α αbase BA, with η = Θ(ηFFT). (5) This decouples the roles of scaling and optimization: αbase restores the suppressed curvature induced by LoRA, while α serves as lightweight tuning knob that can be safely fixed to 1 by default. If further adjustment is needed, α can be searched within narrow and intuitive range (e.g., α [0.1, 10]), eliminating the need for expensive joint tuning over scaling factors and learning rates. We provide two complementary strategies for determining αbase: Variant I: Empirical Scaling for LLMs. Modern LLMs exhibit strong architectural homogeneity, with hidden dimensions typically on the order of thousands (e.g., din = 4096), allowing for universal hyperparameter regime. Leveraging the scaling law from Section 2, we adopt = 256 as robust default, yielding: αbase = 256 Variant II: Analytic Scaling. For general architectures, we derive principled scaling from Corollary 1 by matching the expected task curvature at initialization: αbase = 1 r. This closed-form σA solution provides theoretical baseline that aligns LoRA with the hyperparameter regime of FFT. r. Importantly, Variant serves as global empirical heuristic, while Variant II provides layer-wise theoretical initialization. In practice, the empirical scaling in Variant yields marginally larger magnitudes than the analytic prediction of Variant II for modern LLMs. detailed magnitude comparison is provided in Appendix F. We identify this gap as an important empirical finding. plausible explanation is that the analytic scaling matches the average curvature via the trace of the Hessian, whereas optimization stability is governed by the largest eigenvalue. As shown in Fig. 8(a-b), these quantities can differ within moderate range, making such discrepancies reasonable. Ultimately, this principled choice resolves the 16256 under-scaling inherent in prevailing heuristics.

sections.title: 5 Empirical Validation of LoRA-α
sections.content: To rigorously evaluate the effectiveness of LoRA-α, we conduct extensive experiments along three dimensions: (i) Model diversity, covering encoder, decoder, diffusion, and vision-language architectures; (ii) Task heterogeneity, including natural language and multimodal tasks, encompassing cross entropy, reward-based, flow matching and contrastive learning objectives; (iii) Training horizon, ranging from few-shot adaptation to long-horizon optimization. This comprehensive design validates LoRA-α across both adaptation benchmarks and post-training scenarios. Specifically, we verify the effectiveness of Analytic Scaling on diverse architectures in Section 5.1, while adopting Empirical Scaling as the default configuration for the remaining LLM-centric experiments. All experiments are implemented using the PEFT library [Mangrulkar et al., 2023] and executed on eight NVIDIA H800 GPUs. Due to space limitations, we briefly present the core settings and main results in the following subsections. We benchmark our approach against vanilla LoRA [Hu et al., 2022] (α = r) and representative methods, including scaling-based RsLoRA [Kalajdzievski, 2023], learning-rate-modified LoRA+ [Hayou et al., 2024b], spectral-initialized PiSSA [Meng et al., 2024], and magnitudeinitialized LoRAM [Zhang et al., 2025b]. To ensure fair comparison, we adopt the baseline results from Zhang et al. [2025b], independently training our method under identical settings. Natural Language Understanding (NLU). At first, we fine-tune and evaluate DeBERTa-v3-base184M [He et al., 2023] on eight datasets from the GLUE benchmark [Wang et al., 2018]. All methods are trained with batch size of 32, learning rate of 1 104, and LoRA rank = 8 for 35 epochs. As shown in Table 1, LoRA-α achieves the strongest or near-strongest performance across tasks, outperforming both vanilla LoRA and alternative scaling strategies in average performance. Natural Language Generation (NLG). We then fine-tune Llama 2-7B [Meta Team, 2023] on three datasets covering mathematical reasoning (MetaMathQA), code generation (CodeFeedback), and commonsense reasoning (Commonsense170K), respectively. Training is conducted with batch size of 128, learning rate of 2 105, and LoRA ranks {16, 128} for one epoch over 100k samples. As shown in Table 2, LoRA-α matches or surpasses existing LoRA variants across all tasks and rank settings. The advantage becomes more pronounced at higher rank of 128, indicating that proper α scaling is increasingly critical as model capacity grows. Text-to-Image Synthesis. To validate scalability to multimodal generative settings, we fine-tune Flux.1-12B [Labs, 2024] using the DreamBooth protocol [Ruiz et al., 2023]. All methods are trained with batch size of 1, learning rate of 1 104, and LoRA rank = 8 for 1,000 training steps, supervised by flow matching loss. As illustrated in Fig. 3, LoRA-α produces images with higher fidelity to reference concepts compared to PiSSA and LoRAM, demonstrating that improved scaling generalizes beyond language tasks to complex generative domains. Comparison of α-scaling and η-scaling. We perform hyperparameter sweeps over both α and η on NLU and NLG tasks, with full results provided in Appendix H. As reported in Tables 1 and 2, αscaling consistently yields superior performance compared to η-scaling. Moreover, the performance gap widens significantly at larger ranks, corroborating the initial scaling findings from Fig. 1. 5.2 Performance on Multimodal Representation Learning Moving beyond short-horizon tasks, we evaluate LoRA-α in long-horizon representation learning setting by transforming multimodal LLM into dense retriever on the MMEB benchmark [Jiang et al., 2025]. Following [HuggingFace Team, 2025], we post-tune Qwen 2.5-Math-7B [Qwen Team, 2024] on the Mixture-of-Thoughts dataset including 350k reasoning traces across math, programming, and science with 32k token context. Training uses batch size of 128, learning rate of 4 105, and LoRA ranks {64, 256} for one epoch, taking 2 days per run. We compare FFT, standard LoRA, LoRA with 10 learning rate, and LoRA-α on AIME 2024/2025, MATH-500, GPQA Diamond, and LiveCodeBench v4 using Pass@1. Results. As shown in Table 4, LoRA-α performs comparably to the 10 learning rate variant at smaller ranks, but achieves stronger gains as the rank increases. In contrast, learning-rate scaling becomes unstable at higher ranks. Notably, due to the intrinsic difficulty of this task, LoRA-based methods do not surpass FFT, reflecting the substantial learning capacity required. Nevertheless, LoRA-α consistently emerges as the closest approximation to FFT. 5.4 Performance on Reinforcement Learning Paradigm We evaluate LoRA-α under the reinforcement learning using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) [DeepSeek Team, 2025]. Following [Yin et al., 2025], experiments are conducted on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen (1.5B and 7B) models, trained on the DAPO-Math-17k dataset [Yu et al., 2025] with LoRA rank of 64, learning rate of 1 106 and batch size of 128. We use maximum completion length of 16k tokens with = 8 rollouts per prompt, taking 2 days per run. Evaluation is conducted on suite of challenging mathematical benchmarks, using Pass@1 as the primary metric. Results. As shown in Table 5, LoRA-α achieves the best overall performance across both model scales. In contrast, increasing the learning rate fails to yield reliable gains and often degrades performance, highlighting the instability induced by high-variance policy gradients. FFT also exhibits inconsistent behavior, with noticeable degradation on several benchmarks, suggesting that updating all parameters can disrupt pretrained reasoning structures. Combined with the results in Table 4, these findings echo the observations of [Schulman & Lab, 2025] and suggest broader implication: compared to SFT, reinforcement learning typically requires lower learning capacity while being more susceptible to optimization instability. In this regime, LoRA is better suited than FFT, and properly scaling α preserves training stability and improves learning effectiveness.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: This paper investigates the scaling mechanisms of LoRA, combining extensive empirical evidence with novel Signal-Drift framework. We show that LoRAs stability stems from spectral suppression of the task Hessian, which creates significant optimization gap. Crucially, we demonstrate that scaling α is more effective than increasing the learning rate, as it amplifies task-aligned signal without introducing additional drift. Building on this insight, we establish sublinear scaling law and propose LoRA-α, which restores α to its principled regime and enables direct reuse of FFT learning rates to substantially improve LoRA performance. Overall, our work provides both theoretical insight and practical guidance for unlocking LoRAs potential through simple yet effective scaling mechanism. Looking ahead, developing novel adapter architectures that structurally mitigate this drift without compromising task-signal integrity remains an exciting frontier for future research.

sections.title: A Limitations
sections.content: Our analysis of the asymmetric roles of the scaling factor α and the learning rate η is grounded in the behavior of adaptive optimizers. While the SignalDrift decomposition itself is optimizer-agnostic, the observed asymmetry, where α amplifies task-aligned signal while η introduces additional drift, may not directly extend to vanilla SGD. We adopt this perspective to reflect practical training regimes, where adaptive optimizers are ubiquitous in deep learning and large model training.

sections.title: B Broader Impact
sections.content: This work studies the scaling mechanism of LoRA and proposes principled scaling strategy that improves training effectiveness. By enabling more effective fine-tuning and reduced hyperparameter search, our method can lower the computational cost associated with adapting large models, potentially making such techniques more accessible. At the same time, improved training effectiveness may facilitate the broader deployment of large language models, including in settings where misuse is possible. Nevertheless, our work does not introduce new capabilities beyond improving optimization, and thus does not fundamentally alter the risk profile of existing models. Overall, we view this work as step toward more efficient and principled model adaptation, while emphasizing that downstream applications should be developed and deployed with appropriate safeguards.

sections.title: C Related Work
sections.content: LoRA [Hu et al., 2022] leverages low-rank decomposition to efficiently adapt large models, and has become key technique for scalable deployment in modern large-scale systems, significantly reducing training [Mangrulkar et al., 2023; Schulman & Lab, 2025; Wang et al., 2026] and serving costs [Kwon et al., 2023]. Comprehensive overviews of LoRA and its extensions can be found in [Yang et al., 2024; Mao et al., 2025; He et al., 2026]. To improve the expressiveness of low-rank updates, prior works explore architectural enhancements such as stacking strategies [Lialin et al., 2024; Xia et al., 2024], higher-order parameterizations [Edalati et al., 2023], and customized adapter designs [Jiang et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2024; Zhong et al., 2024]. More relevant to our work, research focuses on improving optimization and training efficiency. Existing approaches investigate scaling strategies [Kalajdzievski, 2023; Biderman et al., 2024], learning rate design [Hayou et al., 2024b], initialization schemes [Meng et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024b; Zhang et al., 2025b], optimizer choices [Li et al., 2025], and batch size configurations [Lee & Lee, 2026]. Optimization Dynamics of LoRA. The optimization behavior of LoRA remains poorly understood due to its bilinear parameterization and non-convex landscape [Li et al., 2025; Xu et al., 2025]. Existing theoretical analyses are often restricted to simplified regimes, such as lazy training or infinite-width limits [Malladi et al., 2023; Hayou et al., 2024a]. Kalajdzievski [2023]; Liu et al. [2025] study stability conditions, showing that improper scaling can lead to gradient collapse or inefficient training. [Hayou et al., 2024b; Zhu et al., 2024] further highlight structural asymmetries between the two low-rank factors, motivating asymmetric optimization strategies such as distinct learning rates. [Mu & Klabjan, 2025] studies non-asymptotic convergence analysis of the original LoRA gradient descent algorithm. While these efforts provide valuable insights, they do not fully explain how scaling choices affect optimization efficiency in practical settings. The ηα Relationship. In practice, LoRA is typically trained with fixed scaling factor (often α r) while tuning the learning rate η [Hu et al., 2022]. Empirically, LoRA requires significantly larger learning rates, often exceeding 10 those of full fine-tuning, to achieve competitive performance [Biderman et al., 2024; Schulman & Lab, 2025]. Recent theoretical results reveal coupling between η and α [Zhang et al., 2025b; Schulman & Lab, 2025], showing that increasing α can mimic the effect of simultaneously scaling the learning rate and initialization under adaptive optimizers. We are inspired by these theoretical analyses, which we interpret as hint that α and η are not interchangeable, with α exerting stronger influence on optimization than the learning rate. More recently, Chen et al. [2026] propose the Maximal-Update Adaptation framework to study rank-invariant learning rates, while Lee et al. [2026] show that different LoRA variants favor distinct learning rate ranges despite achieving similar peak performance. Nevertheless, these studies are largely confined to the regime α = r. Our empirical results show that this constraint limits LoRAs fitting capacity and leaves significant optimization gap. 14 Discussion. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to systematically characterize the role of the scaling factor α in LoRA optimization. While existing analyses emphasize learning rate or initialization, we demonstrate that α plays fundamentally different role. From Hessian perspective, we explain why LoRA requires aggressive hyperparameters, and reveal key asymmetry: α primarily amplifies task-aligned curvature, whereas η amplifies the magnitude of bilinear-induced drift. This decomposition suggests an optimal regime that increases α while keeping η conservative, enabling stronger signal amplification without destabilizing optimization.

sections.title: D Details for Empirical Study
sections.content: In this section, we provide comprehensive details of the experimental setup and extended optimization results across different model scales and datasets. D.1 Experimental Setup and Search Strategy Hyperparameter Search Strategy. To characterize the optimization landscape of LoRA, we tailored our hyperparameter search strategies based on the dataset and our evolving empirical understanding: Tulu 3: Given the lack of established heuristics for the optimal scaling regime at the onset of our study, we adopted two-stage approach for the Tulu 3 dataset. We first defined broad, coarsegrained grid across the learning rate η and the scaling factor α. Upon identifying the general region of convergence, we conducted finer-grained localized search around the minimum validation loss to accurately pinpoint the optimal configurations η and α. OpenThoughts: For the OpenThoughts dataset, rather than repeating an exhaustive grid search, we leveraged the empirical law derived from the Tulu 3 experiments, where the base scaling coefficient was identified near = 256. We anchored our search space around this empirical baseline, systematically sweeping α within 10 range (both scaling up and down) to validate the transferability of the sublinear scaling law. OpenThoughts Dataset Details. To evaluate whether our findings generalize to reasoning-heavy tasks, which typically exhibit different optimization dynamics than general instruction tuning, we utilized the OpenThoughts dataset [Guha et al., 2026]. We sampled 10k training subset and 1k validation set. Because reasoning traces inherently require substantially more context, we extended the maximum sequence length to = 8192 (compared to = 1024 for Tulu 3). Note that FFT experiments were omitted for OpenThoughts, as the primary objective here is to validate the relative scaling dynamics between α and η established in the main text. Figure 4: Hyperparameter analysis of Llama 3-8B on the Tulu 3 dataset. (a) Evaluation loss as function of the learning rate η across different ranks and scaling factors α. Gray lines denote linear fits of the minimum loss for each (r, α). Increasing α lowers the optimal loss and shifts η downward. (b) Scaling paths defined in Eq. (2) relative to the baseline (ηFFT = 1 105, α0 = 16), where η0 denotes the optimal FFT learning rate. Varying α reaches lower-loss regimes that are inaccessible via η-tuning alone. (c) Optimal scaling factor α as function of rank r, evaluated at η0. The observed sublinear trend and large magnitude of α challenge conventional scaling heuristics. 15 Figure 5: Optimization dynamics and α sensitivity for Llama 3-8B on the Tulu 3 dataset over training steps. (a) Training loss and (b) Evaluation loss curves for various combinations of rank r, scaling factor α, and learning rate η, compared alongside FFT. Properly scaled configurations (e.g., larger α with standard η) tightly approximate the FFT trajectory. (c) Sensitivity analysis of the scaling factor α, illustrating how increasing α smoothly accelerates convergence. D.2 Scaling Behavior of Llama 3-8B on Tulu 3 Fig. 4 illustrates the hyperparameter analysis for the Llama 3-8B model on the Tulu 3 dataset. The observations strictly corroborate our findings from the 1B model. Notably, α-scaling continues to demonstrate superior optimization capacity over η-scaling. The optimal scaling factor α fits r, confirming that larger models also operate optimally at an α sublinear relationship α = 512 magnitude substantially larger than conventional rank-tied heuristics. Furthermore, Fig. 5 provides granular view of the optimization dynamics over training steps. The loss trajectories demonstrate that properly scaled configurations, i.e., operating with large α and standard small η, tightly approximate the descent trajectory of FFT. D.3 Validation on OpenThoughts Reasoning Tasks Fig. 6 and 7 present the optimization behaviors on the OpenThoughts dataset for Llama 3-1B and 8B, respectively. Despite the increased sequence length and task complexity, the fundamental asymmetry between α and η remains persistent. Both models consistently avoid early saturation and reach deeper loss minima when α is scaled. Interestingly, the fitted optimal scaling laws yield large coefficients = 1024, indicating that complex reasoning tasks may demand an even more aggressive restoration of the task signal curvature. Figure 6: Hyperparameter analysis of Llama 3-1B on the OpenThoughts dataset with ηFFT = 1105 and α0 = 16. (a) Scaling paths comparing α-scaling and η-scaling. Consistent with the main findings, increasing α enables the model to reach deeper loss minima that are inaccessible via η-tuning alone. (b) Optimal scaling factor α as function of rank r. The fitted sublinear relationship confirms the scaling law established in the main text, demonstrating its robustness across different datasets. 16 Figure 7: Hyperparameter analysis of Llama 3-8B on the OpenThoughts dataset with ηFFT = 1105 and α0 = 16. (a) Scaling paths comparing α-scaling and η-scaling. Consistent with the main findings, increasing α enables the model to reach deeper loss minima that are inaccessible via η-tuning alone. (b) Optimal scaling factor α as function of rank r. The fitted sublinear relationship confirms the scaling law established in the main text, demonstrating its robustness across different datasets. Empirical Validation of Signal-Drift Dynamics To empirically validate the Signal-Drift dynamics and visualize the intractable Hessian matrices without the memory bottleneck, we construct controlled environment using multi-layer perceptron (MLP). This setup allows us to precisely track the optimization trajectories and perform exact spectral analysis on the optimization landscape. Setups. We employ 5-layer MLP utilizing the SiLU activation function, trained to fit synthetic Gaussian data. We generate target projection matrix Wtrue constructed via Singular Value Decomposition (SVD), where the singular values follow strict decay rate of σi = 1/i. The training set consists of = 100 samples drawn from standard normal distribution, with targets generated as = XW true. LoRA adapters are applied to all linear layers with fixed rank of = 16. Pre-trained weights W0 and adapter matrices are initialized from (0, 1/d), while is initialized to zero. For general optimization tracking, we use hidden dimension of = 256. For exact Hessian spectral analysis, which scales quadratically with parameter count, we reduce the dimension to = 32. Optimization Dynamics (η-scaling vs. α-scaling). The model is optimized using Adam for 100 steps with MSE loss. The base learning rate and base scaling factor are set to η0 = 104 and α0 = 1, respectively. To compare their behavioral differences, we conduct experiments across two distinct regimes: η-scaling: We fix α = α0 and strictly scale the learning rate by factors of {16, 32, 64, 128} η0. α-scaling: We anchor the learning rate at η = η0 and scale α by factors of {32, 64, 128, 256} α0. These two regimes yield fundamentally different optimization dynamics. While configurations η = 64η0 and α = 128α0 initially exhibit comparable convergence trends, the η-scaled trajectory already manifests noticeable oscillations. Pushing the scaling further exposes the structural bottleneck: η = 128η0 causes the training to catastrophically diverge, whereas α = 256α0 remains strictly stable and continues to unlock deeper fitting (as evidenced in Fig. 2). Spectral Compression under Low-Rank Parameterization. To understand why LoRA scales differently from FFT, we explicitly compute the full-parameter Hessian Hℓ, the LoRA Hessian HLoRA, and its components HSignal and HDrift via exact automatic differentiation. As shown in Fig. 8(a), the spectrum of HSignal is compressed version of Hℓ. The dominant eigenvalues are significantly reduced, and the trace explicitly follows Tr(HSignal) α2σ2 Tr(Hℓ). Notably, Fig. 8(a) illustrates that the maximum eigenvalue λmax empirically aligns with the predicted order of magnitude, although it is less strictly constrained than the trace. Since the trace equals the sum of all eigenvalues (Tr(H) = (cid:80) λi), this trace-level compression inherently necessitates suppression of the dominant spectral components. This confirms that low-rank parameterization severely restricts curvature energy, 17 Figure 8: Spectral analysis of Hessian matrices. (a) Comparison between the full-parameter Hessian Hℓ and LoRAs signal Hessian HSignal, illustrating the compression of total curvature energy. (b) The predicted sum of eigenvalues from our Signal-Drift framework closely matches the empirical values across various ranks, validating our theoretical derivation. (c) The exact LoRA Hessian HLoRA is inherently indefinite due to the structural drift HDrift. Increasing α triggers spectral purification, where the spectrum converges toward the positive semi-definite HSignal. (d) Empirical verification of asymmetric scaling: the signal curvature magnitude grows as Θ(α2) while drift noise grows only as Θ(α). Consequently, as α increases, λmax[HLoRA] perfectly aligns with λmax[HSignal]. providing quantitative explanation for why larger α is fundamentally required to compensate for rank-induced suppression. Indefiniteness and Spectral Purification. Unlike the full Hessian, HLoRA is inherently indefinite due to the presence of the structural drift HDrift. As shown in Fig. 8(c), this drift component introduces negative eigenvalues, which destabilize optimization. However, increasing α progressively amplifies the signal term relative to the drift, leading to spectral purification effect: the overall indefinite spectrum becomes increasingly aligned with the positive semi-definite HSignal. Asymmetric Scaling of Signal and Drift. Fig. 8(d) empirically verifies the asymmetric scaling behavior. We observe strict divergence in growth rates: λmax[HSignal] = Θ(α2), λmin[HDrift] = Θ(α). This mismatch guarantees that increasing α disproportionately strengthens the task-aligned curvature while only linearly amplifying the destabilizing drift. As result, the dominant eigenvalue of HLoRA increasingly aligns with that of HSignal. (6) (7) Together, these controlled experiments provide validated spectral explanation for the empirical superiority of α-scaling: (i) low-rank parameterization suppresses curvature magnitude; (ii) α restores signal strength in controlled manner; and (iii) the asymmetric scaling between signal and drift naturally dictates that increasing α, rather than the learning rate, is the principled path to stable and accelerated optimization.

To further illustrate the optimization gap, we provide quantitative comparison between our proposed scaling law and prevailing heuristics in Table 6. Conventional practices typically set α = r, which implicitly confines the scaling factor to severely under-scaled regime as the rank increases. According to our Signal-Drift framework, restoring the curvature energy (Hessian trace) to level comparable to FFT requires α to scale with 1 r. For typical model dimension din = 4096 and σ2 = 1/(3din), the theoretical coefficient σA 1 is approximately 111. Our empirical default = 256 is an even more optimized choice that σA ensures deep fitting. As shown in the table, for standard rank = 8, LoRA-α provides scaling magnitude that is 90.5 larger than the common heuristic. Even at = 256, the gap remains as high as 16. This comparison clarifies why standard FFT learning rates have historically been inadequate for driving LoRA to its full potential under legacy scaling rules. Note on Initialization Variance. The parameter σ2 = 1/(3din) is chosen to align with the default initialization protocol in standard deep learning libraries (e.g., PyTorch and PEFT). Specifically, LoRA adapters are typically initialized using Kaiming uniform distribution U((cid:112)1/din, (cid:112)1/din). For uniform distribution U(k, k), the variance is given by k2/3. By substituting k2 = 1/din, we obtain σ2 = 1/(3din). This ensures that the magnitudes predicted by our theoretical framework are consistent in order of magnitude with those used in practical implementations.

sections.title: H Experimental Details
sections.content: We summarize the core configurations and hyperparameters across all six evaluation domains in Table 7. Detailed learning rate schedules and environmental configurations strictly follow the referenced official codebases. Subsequent subsections provide granular details, including datasets and evaluation metrics, for each specific task. H.1 Natural Language Understanding Models and Datasets. We evaluate NLU performance using the DeBERTa-v3-base model (184M) [He et al., 2023] on eight tasks from the GLUE benchmark [Wang et al., 2018]. Following standard protocols, we include MNLI, SST-2, MRPC, CoLA, QNLI, QQP, RTE, and STS-B. LoRA Configuration. All parameter-efficient methods are implemented with fixed rank = 8. For the proposed LoRA-α, we apply the analytic scaling (Variant II) to determine αbase. For other baselines, the scaling factor α follows their respective original designs. Adapters are uniformly applied to all linear layers. Optimization. All models are trained using the AdamW optimizer with constant learning rate of 1 104, aligned with standard FFT settings. Following the PiSSA training framework [Meng et al., 2024], each task strictly utilizes sampled subset of 100k examples to ensure balanced and computationally equivalent comparison. LoRA Configuration. We evaluate performance across two distinct rank regimes: {16, 128}. The detailed, per-task performance breakdowns are provided in Table 9 and Table 10. Given the massive scale of this hyperparameter search space, all results reported in these tables are based on single experimental run per configuration. NLU Grid Search  (Table 9)  : We observe that strictly scaling the learning rate (η) often leads to suboptimal plateaus or local instability (e.g., initial failure to converge on CoLA). In contrast, scaling the structural multiplier α unlocks higher performance ceilings across almost all datasets, raising the aggregated peak performance (LoRA-α) to 89.03, compared to 88.53 for η-scaling. NLG Grid Search  (Table 10)  : The structural bottleneck of η-scaling becomes critically evident, especially at larger ranks (r = 128). Pushing η to extremes triggers catastrophic divergence (e.g., performance collapsing to 0.00 on MATH and GSM8K). Conversely, α-scaling preserves optimization stability under the same aggressive scaling regimes, successfully driving the model to deeper local minima and extending the frontier significantly (averaging 44.87 vs. 42.69). Ultimately, aggregating the optimal configurations explicitly demonstrates that amplifying the signal via α maintains optimization advantage. 27 H.4 Text-to-Image Synthesis Models and Datasets. We evaluate multimodal generation using the Flux.1-12B model [Labs, 2024] for image customization via the DreamBooth protocol [Ruiz et al., 2023]. The implementation is based on the official PEFT DreamBooth examples. The model learns novel concepts from small sets of instance images (typically 46) to generate high-fidelity, prompt-conditioned images. LoRA Configuration. For LoRA-α, we apply analytic scaling (Variant II) to calibrate αbase based on the Flux architecture, with fixed rank of = 8. Adapters are applied to all attention layers Optimization. Models are optimized using AdamW with constant learning rate of 1 104 and batch size of 1. Training is conducted for 1,000 steps. This setting provides challenging environment to test whether α-scaling can accelerate concept acquisition without over-fitting. Evaluation. Following DreamBooth, we use the unique identifier "sks" for concept binding. To test the models ability to preserve the learned concept under novel contexts, evaluation prompts include category and scene descriptions: "A sks <object_name> in sunlit forest, vibrant foliage, ultra-realistic photography" "A sks <object_name>, centered on pure white background, ultra-realistic photography". and H.5 Multimodal Representation Learning Models and Datasets. We evaluate LoRA-α on the MMEB benchmark [Jiang et al., 2025], which converts generative models into dense retrievers. We use Qwen 2-VL (2B and 7B) [Wang et al., 2024a] as backbones, implementing the contrastive training pipeline based on the VLM2Vec codebase. The dataset includes 36 subsets; 20 in-distribution (ID) subsets are used for training, while 16 out-ofdistribution (OOD) subsets are reserved for zero-shot evaluation. LoRA Configuration. For LoRA-α, we adopt Variant with αbase = 256 to all linear layers with fixed rank of = 16. r. Adapters are applied Optimization. Training employs the InfoNCE contrastive loss (temperature 0.02) and the AdamW optimizer. We use global batch size of 1,024 and learning rate of 2 105. Training lasts for 2,000 steps with gradient caching enabled. Evaluation. Representations are extracted from the final-token hidden state. Performance is measured via Precision@1 averaged across ID and OOD splits. This setup rigorously tests the models ability to maintain pretrained multimodal alignment while adapting to discriminative objectives. H.6 Experimental Details for Reasoning-based SFT Models and Datasets. We employ Qwen 2.5-Math-7B [Qwen Team, 2024] as the backbone for long-context reasoning SFT. Training is conducted on the Mixture-of-Thoughts dataset (350k samples) [HuggingFace Team, 2025] utilizing the training recipes provided by Open-R1. LoRA Configuration. We apply adapters to all linear layers with ranks {64, 256}. Furthermore, to support the generation of special tokens, we additionally unfreeze and train the embedding and output layers. LoRA-α uses Variant scaling. Optimization. Models are trained for 1 epoch using the Adam optimizer. We use peak learning rate of 4.0 105. The effective batch size is 128. We enable gradient checkpointing and set the maximum sequence length to 32,768 tokens. Evaluation. Following the established protocols of Open-R1, we evaluate reasoning performance using the Lighteval framework [Habib et al., 2023]. Our evaluation spans comprehensive suite of challenging benchmarks, including AIME 24/25 [MAA, 2024, 2025], MATH-500 [Hendrycks et al., 2021], GPQA Diamond [Rein et al., 2024], and LiveCodeBench v4 [Jain et al., 2025]. Performance is reported as Pass@1 accuracy, where the number of generation samples per prompt strictly adheres to the Open-R1 configuration to ensure standardized and fair comparison across different methods. 28 H.7 Experimental Details for Reasoning-based RL Models and Datasets. We transition to policy optimization using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) [DeepSeek Team, 2025]. We use DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen (1.5B and 7B) as base models, trained on the DAPO-Math-17k dataset [Yu et al., 2025]. We implement the RLVR pipeline based on the PeRL framework [Yin et al., 2025]. This paradigm optimizes reasoning paths using outcome-based rewards. LoRA Configuration. Adapters are applied to all linear layers with rank of = 64. We utilize Variant for LoRA-α. To accelerate rollouts, we use co-located vLLM generation engine [Kwon et al., 2023] with 0.4 memory utilization. Optimization. Training is performed with constant learning rate of 1 106, KL penalty β = 0, and total batch size of 128. For each prompt, we generate = 8 rollouts. The maximum completion length is set to 16,384 tokens to test long-form stability. Evaluation. We assess reasoning performance on MATH-500 [Hendrycks et al., 2021], AMC 23 [MAA, 2023], AIME 24/25 [MAA, 2024, 2025], Minerva [Lewkowycz et al., 2022], and HMMT [Balunovic et al., 2025], using Lighteval framework [Habib et al., 2023]. Performance is reported as Pass@1 accuracy estimated over multiple samples.
